Uyyalavada Narasimha Reddy

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Uyyalavada Narasimha Reddy

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kbaH!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F340b513d-1273-49bb-a871-58b1860a84bd_680x385.png "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Rayalaseema, a dry and semi-arid region in the southern part of Andhra Pradesh, is characterized by its rugged terrain, rocky outcrops, undulating plateaus, and the forested stretches of the Nallamala Hills. Encompassing the districts of Anantapur, Chittoor, Kadapa, and Kurnool, the region derives its name, “Land of Rayalavaaru,” from its historical association with the Vijayanagara Empire, one of South India’s most powerful and influential dynasties.

In its heyday, Rayalaseema was an integral part of this mighty empire. Notably, Penugonda, once the empire’s second capital,rose to prominence after the catastrophic defeat at the Battle of Talikota and the subsequent destruction of Hampi. Later, Chandragiri, also located in Rayalaseema, served as the last capital of the empire, symbolizing its final stand before decline.

The region is also home to the sacred town of Tirupati, renowned for the Tirumala Venkateswara Temple, one of the richest and most visited Hindu temples in the world, dedicated to Lord Venkateswara, also known as Balaji. Rayalaseema also hosts Srisailam, one of the Dwadasa Jyotirlingas, and Sri Kalahasti, revered as one of the Panchabhuta Kshetras, representing the element of air.

Scattered across the landscape are enduring symbols of the region’s glorious past: the imposing forts of Gooty, Gandikota, and Adoni; the exquisitely carved temples of Lepakshi, Pushpagiri, and Tadipatri.all standing as living testaments to the architectural brilliance of the Vijayanagara era.

In 1796, facing mounting pressure from both the Marathas and Tipu Sultan, who had been conducting frequent raids into his territory, Nizam Asaf Jah II of Hyderabad sought British protection. As part of the subsequent Subsidiary Alliance with the British East India Company, he ceded the four districts of Rayalaseema, along with Bellary and parts of Tumkur (in present-day Karnataka), to the British in return for military support.

This transfer of territory came to be known as the Ceded Districts, a term that stuck in colonial records. However, over time, the label “Ceded” has come to be viewed as derogatory and reductive, as it overlooks the region’s rich historical and cultural heritage.

The Palegars (or Polygars) of Rayalaseema, once autonomous feudal chieftains under the Vijayanagara Empire, gradually came under British control following the annexation of the region. The British, eyeing their vast estates and accumulated wealth, began abolishing their traditional privileges, imposing harsh revenue demands and eroding their authority. Amidst this rising discontent, one Palegar would rise in defiance—his name would echo through the hills and plains of Rayalaseema as a symbol of bravery and resistance: Uyyalawada Narasimha Reddy.

Born in Rupanagudi, Narasimha Reddy was the son of Neelamma, the second wife of Pedda Malla Reddy, the Palegar of the estate now located in Kurnool district. Though born in Rupanagudi, he was raised in Uyyalawada, from where he administered a domain that spanned 66 villages across Bellary, Kadapa, Anantapur, and Kurnool districts.

The spark for rebellion was lit in 1846, when the British discontinued a monthly grant of eleven rupees that Narasimha Reddy’s father had long received. Citing a technicality—that Narasimha Reddy was an adopted son and therefore not a legal heir—the colonial authorities denied him the rightful allowance. When he sent his associate to the Koilkuntla treasury to collect the grant, the local Tahsildar not only refused the request but insulted and dismissed him outright.

Adding to this humiliation, the British had already seized Narasimha Reddy’s ancestral property, treating it as forfeited due to the discontinued grant. This high-handed action served as the final provocation. Enraged and betrayed, Narasimha Reddy raised the banner of revolt, determined to fight back against the injustice and exploitation inflicted by the colonial regime.

Narasimha Reddy’s defiance soon ignited a wider rebellion, as other dispossessed Palegars and zamindars, similarly affected by British land seizures and revenue policies, rallied to his cause. Among those who joined the uprising were the zamindars of Wanaparthy, Munagala, and Penugonda, as well as Papa Khan of Kurnool—all united in their resentment against colonial exploitation.

The revolt also found strong grassroots support from the Boya and Chenchu tribes of the Nallamala hills, who had long suffered under British oppression. These tribal communities, harassed by unjust taxes, extortion, and the erosion of their traditional rights, saw in Narasimha Reddy a leader who stood against imperial tyranny. Their intimate knowledge of the forested terrain proved invaluable, and they played a crucial role in assisting the rebel forces.

The first major strike came on July 10, 1846, when Narasimha Reddy, leading a force of over 500 Boya tribal warriors, launched a fierce assault on the British treasury at Koilkuntla. In the ensuing chaos, Hari Singh, the treasurer, was killed, and the building was set ablaze, as Narasimha Reddy reclaimed the money he believed was rightfully his. The bold attack sent shockwaves through the British administration, marking the rebellion as a serious threat.

Alarmed, the British responded with swift and brutal measures. They arrested two of Narasimha Reddy’s closest associates, Gosai Venkanna and Obanna, subjecting them to torture in an attempt to extract confessions and establish his direct role in the treasury raid.

A military detachment under Captain Knott and Watson was dispatched to apprehend Narasimha Reddy. However, in a decisive skirmish, the British forces were defeated and forced to retreat—a stunning setback that further emboldened the rebels and heightened colonial anxieties.

In a desperate attempt to suppress the growing rebellion, the British announced a reward of one thousand rupees for anyone who could capture Narasimha Reddy. Yet, their hopes were dashed once more on July 23, 1846, when a British force marching through the Nallamala forests near Giddalur was ambushed in a daring midnight raid. Narasimha Reddy’s forces routed the colonial troops, forcing them into yet another humiliating retreat.

Emboldened by successive victories, Narasimha Reddy adopted guerrilla tactics, launching a series of hit-and-run attacks, ambushes, and raids that kept the British on edge for months. Frustrated by their inability to capture him in direct combat, the British resorted to psychological warfare—they captured his family members and imprisoned them in Kadapa, hoping this would force him out of hiding.

Their strategy worked. Upon learning of his family’s imprisonment, Narasimha Reddy made his way toward Kadapa in an attempt to rescue them. However, Collector Thomas Cochrane, having caught wind of his presence, learned that Narasimha Reddy had taken refuge at the Jagannatha Temple at Perusomala, deep in the Nallamala hills. Wasting no time, Cochrane mobilized his forces and surrounded the temple, closing the trap on the elusive rebel leader.

The British bribed his cook to mix toddy in his meal, making him intoxicated, while his gun was disabled, as he was captured captured on October 6, 1846, and bought to Koilkuntla, along with 900 of his associates. While around four hundred were left free, more than two hundred and seventy were freed on bail, while about one hundred and twelve were imprisoned. After a long trial, he was deemed guilty of waging war against the Government, as well as loot and murder. Finally on February 22, 1847, around 7 AM, Uyyalavada Narasimha Reddy was publicly hanged at Jurreti, his head was put on display at the Koilkuntla fort, as a warning to the public against revolt.
